speaker: interviewer
text: September 6, 1995. Survivor, Michael Kishel-- Kisielnicki. Interviewer, Naomi Rappaport. City, Forest Hills. State, New York. Country, USA. Language, English. My name is Naomi Rappaport. And the date is September the 6th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Michael Kishel in Forest Hills, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd like to begin by asking you to give us your name and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name in the United States is Michael Kishel, M-I-C-H-A-E-L K-I-S-H-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Majlech Kisielnicki. I say it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say it Majlech because it depends where you come from, whether you are a-- a Galitzianer or a Litvak. And I spell Majlech as M-A-J-L-E-C-H. Kisielnicki was K-I-S-I-E-L-N-I-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a name during the war time when I had to get myself a passport. And the name was Miet Mieczyslaw the first name, but I don't remember what name I was second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell Mieczyslaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-E-C-Z-Y-S-L-A-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Mietek was in during the war time, they called me at that time on the-- when I had the-- the-- as a-- as an Aryan-- Mietek, M-I-E-T-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bir-- I was born on August 18, 1920, in Kalushin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the present, I'm 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Kalushin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalushin in Polish was spelled K-A-L-U-S-C-Y-N. Here, we'd spell it Kalushin K-A-L-U-S-H-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Poland, in state of Warsaw, or the county of Minsk Mazowiecki. County was in Polish is Powiat, P-O-W-I-A-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for allowing us to come in and talk with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to start with your family and where-- where they were born, and whether they got-- how they got to Kalushin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My family was born in Kalushin and their forefathers. I remember my grandfather, my both grandfathers, they were in Kalushin for many years. As a matter of fact, I remember my great grandmother from my mother's side. When she died-- before she died, she was laying in bed for quite a long time. She was about 100 years-- I believe 105 years old when she died. I've seen her often when I came to visit my grandmother, as there was the place where she lived. And as far as I know, they're all in Kalushin for many, many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents? My father's name was Mosha and my mother's name was Razel. And Razel maiden name was Rzansky. In Poland, we spelled it Rzansky, R-Z-A-- with a sign-- N-S-K-Y. Here, I spell Rzansky Z-O-N-Z-I-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your gran-- you say you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father's father was [INAUDIBLE] Kisielnicki. And my father's mother was Es-- Es-- Esther. And my mother's father was [? Fischel. ?] And my mother's mother was Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did paren-- your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. We had a grocery, wholesale grocery and retail grocery. That was in the late-- I remember-- in the late '20s, also in the early '30s. Later on, we had made the gasoline station. And we also with a partnership in trucks that the people in the town needed to have transportation, they needed support. My father was a partner, like a silent partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- he sold trucks, or he rented them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we did not rent trucks. We did not sell. We were a partner to the-- to their trucking. There were a group of people that used to carry merchandise to and from Warsaw, and they needed to-- the money or they needed things, and my father was like a silent partner. Then investing in it. And-- and as a matter of fact, that my uncle, my father's brother, was a partner in that endeavor. This is how we had the trucks also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other-- he was in other businesses as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, is the-- Yes. We had a restaurant. We had a restaurant that was open 24 hours a day with the exception of Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and New Year-- Christmas and New Year's. Well, the Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur was closed altogether. But otherwise Friday night, because it was a religious town, was open the entrance from the back, from the side street, because it was Friday and we did not want to antagonize people-- the neighbors or other people because the entrance of the business was done on the side of us. And secondly, business on Fridays was almost nil because the work was mostly the customers that we have that were traveling from far east, from Brest, from [INAUDIBLE], from Baranovichi, from Lodz and Rovno and Vilna, had to pass by our town because it was the main route from Warsaw to Moscow, to say it. And they all stopped, used both gasoline and came to the restaurant. And the same thing was with buses. There were buses that are going to Warsaw. But just like have long distance buses here, and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of restaurant was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a restaurant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kosher restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kosher. Yes. In our town, there was no such thing as non-kosher. Whoever wanted to eat non-kosher had to hide and go to a-- to a store that sold non-kosher meat, which was kielbasa, and some other meat, that no one should see you. So the-- the town was a 10-- population of 10,000 people with about 65% to 70% Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What was the town specialty? What did people earn their living from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The specialty of town were- were a they use to manufacture of tallits, prayer shawls. And there was a factory, a few-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] produced leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tannery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tanneries. A few people that had tanners, different things. And were also a big factory that produces sheep clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sheep skin, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheep skin coats. Not only sheep skin. They used to buy the sheepskins from overseas. Not overseas, from Europe, other places, to have enough to produce. So they used to go-- I know because some-- some of them are friends of mine. They used to go to Yugoslavia. They used to go to Greece. To places that they had a lot of sheep. They bought the sheep, the skin, there and produced sheepskin coats that the villagers, the farmers used to buy. And also, the population of the town and other places used to use it wintertime because the-- the-- the wool-- the skin was underneath and the wool was on the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Kalushin considered a wealthy Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No. Well, by wealthy, I would say that there were a lot of wealthy people, but were more poor people. But being that everyone takes care of their own. So we lived-- we lived and let live, and everybody was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your own family? Were they considered wealthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were considered Kalushin wealthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about your own home? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- actually, we had a house that consisted of the store and the restaurant. And upstairs, we had a bedroom. But we did not have enough space for the bedroom. We rented a bedroom two houses away so-- because we were three children. I had a brother and a sister. And we had-- we had help, two helps, two maids. And we needed to have space for them. So we could say there was like-- here would say like a two bedroom apartment because the living-- there was no such thing as a living room. There were couches, but not exactly like here because there were no television to see. And the dining room was the main dining room that used to be used for the restaurant. And the kitchen had a stove, a coal stove. And that was it. We had a backyard with a lot of-- to use like a warehouse. As I mentioned, my father was in the wholesale groceries, in the wholesale supplies. That we sold coal. We sold naphtha-- naphtha is kerosene-- for the farmers. And that was kept in the warehouse in the-- in the back of-- in the yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you rented rooms for people, for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the children to sleep in?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the children to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents slept in the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: They slept in the bedroom because we were open 24 hours a day. So some peo-- some of us slept during the daytime or the morning till noon, or some slept later. And we used both bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And eating. You ate in the restaurant--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at all times?

speaker: interviewee
text: At all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: At all times. So you ate in a separate place, or you ate in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: --restaurant along with all the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --guests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depending the time, because dinnertime was there 12 o'clock. Like we have lunch here, there was the main meal was done at that time. And at that time, there wasn't-- it wasn't busy because most the busy time in the restaurant and in the stores and the gasoline were in the morning. I'd say from three, four o'clock in the morning till about eight, nine o'clock was busy because all the buses and trucks passed by and they brought the business. And they start to return around 5:00, 6:00 in the afternoon till about 10:00, 11:00 at night and later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about during holidays and shabbat? Did you still eat in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No. If the store was closed. The front was closed. And we still had-- the restaurant was not in the front. The restaurant was in the middle of the house. You went in, it was the store. After the store was the kitchen. And after the kitchen was a big long, big table. Table like this size. And that's where we ate. And behind that room was a bedroom. Behind the dining room was a bedroom. And we also had upstairs room that we used to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how would you celebrate a holiday? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: --invite the strangers to join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There used to be strangers-- there used to be strangers to come in-- let's see, to say like you say here, beggars. They used to go on their-- they used to go from town to town and ask for donations. And sometimes, a-- a beggar passed-- passed by and didn't have any place to sleep, so my father used to occasionally to invite them. But they were most of the time already people that my father knew. They needed to sleep overnight or-- or have a meal. There was always an open door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your neighbors, who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we had-- a matter of fact, we had also Polish neighbors because the mi-- the town was 70% Jewish. The outskirts of the town was occupied mostly by Gentile, by Poles. Our house was almost the last house in town on the way to Warsaw. And the next neighbor had another restaurant, but it had like a meat place they used to sell non-kosher food. So that was the last house. And then there was an-- an orchard. A man-- but this was not occupied on the side, not on the main street. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to-- did y ou go to a Polish school?

words.text: go to-- did y ou go

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went. At the beginning, I was three, four years old, I went to Cheder. At six or six and something-- it depends the time. I think I was six. I went to public school, which was school for all children, Polish, teaching us Polish and so on. When later on I went to three schools. In the morning, from let's say from 9:00 to 11:00, or 9:00 to 12:00, I went to Hebrew school teaching me just Hebrew. Then I went to public school for the-- to teach everything else. And in the evening, a rabbi used to come to the house to teach me for bar mitzvah and so on and so forth. So it was a day of school in--

speaker: interviewer
text: At home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you went to Polish public school, was that just for Jewish children or for Polish children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were two types of schools. See, mostly the pe-- the Jews in our town spoke Yiddish. So for them to speak Polish, they had a very bad accent. My Polish was so good that I could go on and on, and nobody knew as I was a Pole, because even when I was during the wartime, I was as a Pole. No-- I was blonde, not the hair that I have now, and blue eyes. And I looked like a Pole. My speech was undetectable, and my speech, my Pole, it was perfect. And so you-- I went only to a Jewish-Polish school. Other-- other students that had very heavy accent had to go to a Polish mixed school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get-- to lose the accent and to get more knowledge to speak less Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who were your friends in school? In school, boys like-- like myself. Matter of fact, two of my friends still live in Israel-- not still live in Israel. One ran away to Russia, and there one was-- lived through the occupation, and they live in Israel there. One friend of mine is here, which was in Russia. She came from Russia and she immigrated to the United States. Another friend of mine that survived the war lived in France, and she died about a year and a half ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. We had friends, but we did not go- go steady. I mean, we said hello to us and we were friendly with them because we could not be that hard. But I could say I had some non-Jewish friends, but not as-- not as well or as many as Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit in each other's houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Especially when it came Christmas time, the Gentile neighbors used to invite us to come to see the Christmas trees. And they used to come when it was holidays, used to challah to taste or some fish. We were mostly friendly. But separately, the relations were not too hot. And us especially, we did not have any problem because we had a business. And mostly the precinct, there were four or five policemen-- four-- probably four. And mostly, they were stationed almost in our house because they looked to get-- to get paid and they always looked for how to get paid is from truck men that the light didn't was on, or there's this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the pay was a half a pack of cigarettes. The half a pack of cigarettes-- there were 10 cigarettes-- was $0.50, 50 Russian. It was a big deal to them, but during the night they could make quite a few-- quite a few zlotys. Yeah. Did you have any-- do you have any memory of incidents in the relationship between you and some of the Polish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We occasionally used to fight. They used to call us cebula, which means onion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Onion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to call them kapusta, which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cabbage.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cabbage. But especially those-- that was a fight. But no-- no special fight, no ordinary fights like hitting. There might have been somebody hit, but not in the form that you can here-- see it here on televisions, or hear what's going on in places that people attack other ones just because he thinks the other one has money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these names have anti-Semitic overtones? cebula and kapusta?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. Because they-- they-- it-- it was-- it originated much before me. No. Because we knew that we call them kapusta, and they used to call us cebula. Because first of all, the Jews like onions. And maybe they like kapusta. Maybe they liked cabbage. Of course, we had cabbage also. But maybe different tasting and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what was the Kehillah like, the Jewish communal life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish communal life before the war. I remember there was a Kehillah made out of Jewish. And they were only, let's say, reigning or seeing that we support the places of worship. We support our main rabbi, and so on. Matter of fact, I have a picture of one president of the-- of the Kehillah, of the Jewish. But when the-- when the war broke out, the Kehillah disintegrated. And the mayor of the town received an order from the Germans to organize to get 10 Jews to make a Judenrat, like a Kehillah. And they are to be responsible not to the Jews, but responsible to the Germans, whatever the Germans will have over us to place or to want or to do. And my father was one of them to be designated as a member of the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a member of the Kehillah, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who got to be the head of a Kehillah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly religious people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the wealthy people? Did they get to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were some religious wealthy people. There were some. And some-- and some were even not wealthy people. They were just known because maybe their forefathers or their immediate family was more learned than not learned. But there was-- not everyone tried to push himself into being in the Kehillah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How aware were you in the '30s, the late '30s, of the rise of Hitler, and Nazism, and the plight of the German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very well aware of because going to school, and specialty the school, and the newspapers that we read, the Yiddish papers, because we used to get a Jewish paper. From Warsaw, there was [INAUDIBLE]. There were various Yiddish papers, and they were writing. And we had radio. And we heard what happened. And we very much were very well aware. But the propaganda in Poland in the 1930s, let's say, '37, '38 when Hitler started to take in Austria, and then he took Sudeten, and then he started to ask a corridor through [INAUDIBLE]. It's the upper part where [INAUDIBLE], or Danzig. Then we realized that they may ask for something like that, too. But the propaganda in Poland was always like this. Oh, we're not afraid of the Germans. They don't have tanks. Their tanks are made with cardboard, or something like that. They were knocking down their strength of the-- of the Germans, supporting the Polish army, the Polish willpower. And although we did not believe it, but we had no choice. We could not say that it's not so. Otherwise, we would have been-- we would have been traitors. We say, no, we are weak, or something. And we really hoped that this is all what the Polish propaganda says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Kalushin get any German Jews, German-Polish Jews who were sent back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did not get any German Jews. But we did get any Polish Jews from territory that the Germany annexed right after the war. And these came Jews from Kalish. In Yiddish, they call Kalish. And spelling in Polish was K-A-L-Y-S-Z. And in English, it's spelled K-A-L-I-S-H. And also Pabianic, P-A-B-I-A-N-I-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, they are names of places?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are names of towns. One Kalish was a large town. I would say may have been about a half a million people. Pabianic was a smaller town in the area of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the population take to these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not how they took it, because we took them in because their- their town itself, Kalushin was destroyed, the middle of town. And a lot of people moved in with others. And they're saying when they brought those people in, we still had to squeeze a little more and put in more in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was after the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Hitler had-- the Germans had deported Jews from Hamburg and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Cologne--

speaker: interviewee
text: We know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: --way back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in '38, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the traitors, they deported a lot of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of-- none of those Jews were from Kalushin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they did not come to any relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they used to come to Warsaw, they used to come-- But a lot of them came to Warsaw because maybe they had relatives in Warsaw. Maybe they were wealthy and they could set-- settle themselves in Warsaw because they had money. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was in the 19-- I think it was '35 or '36, I don't remember exactly the year, when there was a World Exhibition in France. My father had a sister in France. And the one who took the-- the chance to go to visit her. There were special fares and so on. And he came there and he saw the life altogether different, from a small town, Kalushin, of 10,000 population, to a city like Paris. It's like day in the summer with night in the winter. To-- and he liked it very much. And he said we're going to do-- do-- transfer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by transferring, how do we do that? So there was a lot of relatives from Kalushin had in France. And the French relatives used to send money to their relatives in Kalushin, to the poor relatives. So instead of them sending the money, he used to give the money to my aunt, to my father's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wrote us a letter. And we paid out to the relatives. And this is how we accumulated money in France to go. But then my father came back and he saw what happens here. We have a business, like a growing business like living with all the wealth and all this, this, this. And he start to think again. We're going to do, there's a new language. And again, and with a business man who doesn't-- it doesn't have a trade. My father's brother-in-law was my aunt's brother, was a tailor. And tailoring in France was the main profession. And not just a tailor, he was a good tailor. And so we decided not to go. And that's how we remained in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any groups, any young peoples groups, a Bund Zionist--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any other, any political groups--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were many.

speaker: interviewer
text: --urging you to leave or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no-- the only thing urging to leave was the Zionist organization. There was a Zionist organization, [INAUDIBLE] Zionist. I mean, mostly all the people like my father and other ones belonged to. And there was younger ones and still younger ones. There were the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE]. That was, let's say, socialist minded. All these were urging to go to Israel. At that time, we could not go to Israel because of Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how could we go when the-- it was, as we can say, it was occupied by the British, by English. And they had to give you a permit to go. People used to apply. It took three, four, five years to get it. And then there was the [POLISH], right [POLISH] and left [POLISH]. All those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Socialist.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Zionist minded. Then there was the Bund, which was socialist minded. And they say they don't go any place. We've got to make a socialist country in Poland. Then there was the Communist Party, which was underground, which was not allowed to be. But nevertheless, there were many Jews that belonged to the Communist Party. And although we were Zionist minded-- especially my-- as my brother, my brother belongs to-- but had friends that were Communists. And when it came before the May 1, they-- they-- they used to throw in a flag on the electric wires to show that the Communists still exist. So they used to hide-- my brother used to hide his friends' flags. When it comes the 1st, we'll be able to take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about the German invasion of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the German? Of course, as I mentioned before, we learned about a German invasion right September 1, because they were preparing. Preparing. Because we saw a lot of-- a lot of preparations to-- of the armies, traveling one base to another with their military trucks. And we knew that something was going to happen unless Poland gives up, gives them the break. And being that England and France had guaranteed the Polish border, Poland became strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first day of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will remember the first day. There was a lot of-- a lot of traffic. A lot of people started to run away east, east towards the Russian border. And I remember it because I-- most of the time, I was working nights. And there were so, so many people that I haven't seen. We couldn't handle that many people. People used to take some food out and to eat outside. And the-- and the first day of war, there was a plane passed by. And there was a-- a line to-- in front of a bakery, and a bomb fell nearby. And that was the first time we saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we're going to have to change our tape now.
